utterances: But today the South Dakota House is expected to give final approval to a measure that would ban all abortions in the state, except those needed to save the life of the pregnant woman.
The State Senate, which approved the bill Wednesday, rejected amendments to allow abortions in cases of rape or incest, or to protect the pregnant woman's health.
Unruh says she's confident that Republican Governor Michael Rounds will sign the measure, and she makes it clear that she hopes the law will have a broader impact.
We've been very successful to chip away at the laws of Roe v.
Wade in South Dakota, and we think the rest of the country should really be following us and following the heartland, and this is definitely a plan to go after Roe v.
Wade.
That would be the 1973 Supreme Court decision that legalized abortion nationwide.
Eve Gartner, senior staff attorney for the Planned Parenthood Federation of America, says the only way the South Dakota law could take effect is if the Supreme Court were to throw out Roe.
This law is a direct challenge to Roe v.
Wade, and if this law is upheld, it would mean that abortions could be banned anywhere in this country.
This isn't the first time a state has tried to outlaw abortion since Roe was decided.
Utah, Louisiana and the territory of Guam all passed measures in the late 1980s that were subsequently blocked.
That flurry of state laws came after the Supreme Court signaled that it might be willing to accept more restrictions on abortion.
Gartner says the timing of the South Dakota measure is no coincidence either.
It's coming just as two potentially anti-abortion justices, John Roberts and Samuel Alito, have joined the Court.
Well, good.
Well, let's start, actually, with New York and Senator Hillary Clinton.
She was forced to do some more damage control this week about a surrogate's comments about Senator Barack Obama.
This time it's former Nebraska Senator Bob Kerrey.
Give us details, can you?

Well, Senator Kerrey endorsed Senator Clinton, but in doing so he then went on to elaborate about his admiration for a man he referred to as Barack Hussein Obama and talk about how Barack Hussein Obama had a Muslim father and who has Muslim grandparents, and therefore he would be a wonderful emissary to Muslims in the world and spread goodwill and more understanding of American foreign policy abroad.

Well, of course lots of people picked up on the idea that here is Senator Kerrey emphasizing the Muslim background for Senator Obama at a time when Senator Obama has been making a pointed effort to say he's a Christian.
In fact, if you look at his holiday commercials, it's all about Christian imagery with his family, his two daughters and his wife.

And of course, his critics, and some who are wish him the worse, have been pointing that his middle name is Hussein and asking how such a person could ever be elected president of the United States in this time of the war on terrorism.
And so finally, Bob Kerrey, picking up that it was being perceived in such negative light, apologized.
But I don't think the - from what I'm hearing inside the Obama campaign, they didn't take it as an apology.
They took it as a strategy by a Clinton supporter to again portray Obama as very vulnerable to Republican attacks in the general election.
All right.
Well, at least the Democrats are out campaigning this week.
You can't say that for a former New York Mayor Rudy Giuliani, the Republican who was hospitalized briefly this week, after what were described as flu-like symptoms.
This is kind of a bad day for him to be missing campaigning because he needs to be doing a little catching up now, doesn't he?
Well, he's got a terrific amount of catching - I don't think he can catch up in Iowa or in New Hampshire.
But it would be good for him to raise his profile and that was the intent.
And the second thing is, he needs to raise money.
Recently, he'd been on the road, places like Oklahoma, Texas, but anywhere that he could raise money, because he's going to need a great deal of money as you approach these big states.
And suddenly he's in the hospital.
And it's not just the hospital, it's also been that his numbers - his poll numbers have just been sinking, sinking, sinking, Alex.
Earlier this week I was having a conversation with someone who said he may not win any of the first six Republican contests and therefore it's hard to see him having any momentum.
So a very difficult time because when the conversation was about terrorism, he was doing well.
And it subsumed all the conversation about his social stands on immigration, abortion, gay marriage, all of that.
Now it seems as if there is less conversation about terrorism, more focus on character, and all of the questions about Rudy Giuliani have been sinking his campaign.
Your most interesting political conversation of the week, Juan.
Well, you know, it's a conversation about a conversation, Alex.

And it's about Rush Limbaugh, the talk show host - America's number one talk show host - who said that a picture that he saw on the Matt Drudge Web site of Hillary Clinton looking rather tired and worn, he said, look at her, she's aging on the campaign trail, and nobody, no American is going to want to see a woman as she ages during her term as president of the United States.
Limbaugh has said, hey, you know what, I wasn't trying to insult her.
I was simply - he was simply pointing out that women often times are held to a different standard than men as they grow older and age in public.
But of course the way that it came off, and coming from Rush Limbaugh, was as an attack on Hillary Clinton and a reminder to the American people that she has days when she is looking older.
And it was as if he was in fact holding her to a very different standard than he would hold any male politician.
So it would might have been somewhat tricky and, you know, I think rather snarky, but you know what, he had a defense.
But nonetheless, everybody, again, said this was not a well-intentioned comment by Mr. Limbaugh.
I would just say that Mr. Limbaugh is fortunate to be working in the medium of radio himself.
And I don't know that his comments on the lookism of others are, well...
(Soundbite of laughter)
Juan...
Happy holidays, Alex.
Merry Christmas and Happy New Year.
Singer, songwriter and actor Charles Aznavour has died at the age of 94.
He had often been called the French Frank Sinatra.
Aznavour was an international star who performed around the world.
NPR's Eleanor Beardsley reports he was especially beloved in his native France, where tributes are pouring in.
Charles Aznavour songs are playing nonstop on French radio.
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""LA BOHEME"")
(Singing in French).
The country's president, Emmanuel Macron, tweeted that Aznavour had accompanied the joys and pains of three generations and that his voice and unique brilliance will shine on.
Throughout his 70-year career, Aznavour sold more than a hundred million records in 80 countries.
Jean-Pierre Pasqualini, head of programming at Melody TV, says Aznavour broke taboos.
He was the first singer in the '50s who was sensual.
In the '50s, all the girls had blushes when they listened to Charles Aznavour.
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""HIER ENCORE"")
(Singing in French).
Pasqualini says Aznavour was daring.
The singer composed some 1,400 songs, many about important themes.
He wrote about homosexuality.
In '72, it was very early.
He dared a lot.
Charles Aznavour was born in France in 1924.
His relatives fled the Armenian genocide in Turkey.
Aznavour was discovered by Edith Piaf and wrote songs for her and toured with her after the war.
He had his biggest hit in English in 1974 with ""She.
(SOUNDBITE OF SONG, ""SHE"")
(Singing) Me, I'll take her laughter and her tears and make them all my souvenirs, for where she goes, I've got to be.
The meaning of my life is she.
Late into the night, the cold skies of Pristina crackled with the sound of fireworks alternating with celebratory gun fire.
The new Kosovo flag was reveled: a map of Kosovo on a blue background with six stars, a reference to the minorities living along side the two million ethnic Albanians.
Respect for minority rights was the underlying theme of the declaration of independence.
Prime Minister Hashim Thaci, a former guerilla leader, read out at an extraordinary session of the Kosovo parliament.

(Through translator) Devoted to promoting and respecting the diversity of our people, we, the leaders of our country, elected democratically - a declaration of declaring a state, because Kosovo is an independent state, a democratic state.
(Soundbite of cheering)
Throughout the day, emotions were intense.
(Soundbite of music, horns honking)
Music and car horns echoed through the streets.
(Soundbite of music, horns honking)
Thousands of people strolled along Mother Teresa Boulevard, waving Albanian national flags and cheering to the sound of a constant drum beat.
Thirty-year-old Basa Barani(ph) was overjoyed.
Now I feel good for my future because my children will, I know my children will be born in a state, for example.
They will have much better life than I do, I hope and I dream.
So it's a great, great emotion.
That's all I can say.
The new state faces huge challenges.
It's overpopulated and mired in poverty.
The jobless rate is close to 70 percent, and 20 percent of Kosovo's GNP comes from the presence of international civilians and the 16,000-strong NATO Peace Keeping Force.
Nevertheless, this young ethnic Albanian woman, Shrepa Sela(ph) believes independence will solve all economic problems.
When they woke up in the morning, they were nowhere to go, you know, to support their family.
Not it's like, wake up in the morning and hold their head, like, what's going to happen?
How am I supposed to feed our kids, our families and all that?
